root_text.an3.38:1.1: “Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo deve tāvatiṁse anunayamāno tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.an3.38:3.1: Sā kho panesā, bhikkhave, sakkena devānamindena gāthā duggītā na sugītā dubbhāsitā na subhāsitā.
root_text.an3.38:3.3: Sakko hi, bhikkhave, devānamindo aparimutto jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘aparimutto dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.an3.38:4.1: Yo ca kho so, bhikkhave, bhikkhu arahaṁ khīṇāsavo vusitavā katakaraṇīyo ohitabhāro anuppattasadattho parikkhīṇabhavasaṁyojano sammadaññāvimutto, tassa kho etaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno kallaṁ vacanāya:
root_text.an3.38:6.2: So hi, bhikkhave, bhikkhu parimutto jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘parimutto dukkhasmā’ti vadāmī”ti.

translation_text.an3.38:1.1: “Once upon a time, mendicants, Sakka, lord of gods, guiding the gods of the thirty-three, spoke this verse:
translation_text.an3.38:2.4: on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days,
translation_text.an3.38:2.5: and the eighth day of the fortnight,
translation_text.an3.38:2.6: as well as on the fortnightly special displays.’
translation_text.an3.38:3.1: But that verse was poorly sung by Sakka, lord of gods, not well sung; poorly spoken, not well spoken.
translation_text.an3.38:3.3: Because Sakka, lord of gods, is not exempt from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. He is not exempt from suffering, I say.
translation_text.an3.38:4.1: But for a mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—it is appropriate to say:
translation_text.an3.38:5.4: on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days,
translation_text.an3.38:5.5: and the eighth day of the fortnight,
translation_text.an3.38:5.6: as well as on the fortnightly special displays.’
translation_text.an3.38:6.2: Because that mendicant is exempt from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. He is exempt from suffering, I say.”
